facts_disclosed: Presenting residential portfolio to Al-Faisal family on March 5, 2025

turns.content: I have a presentation in six days that could define the next chapter of my firm. The Al-Faisal family. March 5th. If you understand anything about the landscape here, you will understand what that name represents. I need the presentation to be impeccable, and I want to think through the structure with someone who will challenge me.

That sounds like a defining opportunity, and with six days to go, the focus should be on sharpening and refining rather than building from scratch. You clearly know what you're presenting — the value of this conversation is in pressure-testing the structure.

To help effectively, I need to understand the setup:

1. What format — digital, physical, or a combination?
2. Who will be in the room — family members, advisors, an architect?
3. Is this for a specific project, or are they evaluating your firm generally?

A combination. Digital presentation on a large screen for the portfolio — project photography, plans, concept narratives. Physical material samples on the table so they can touch our palette. And printed portfolio books as leave-behinds, one for each person present. I expect the matriarch and two of her sons. No architect — they are selecting the interior designer before the architect, which tells me they value the interiors as the primary experience of the home, not the shell. That is favourable.

Very favourable. Choosing the interior designer before the architect signals that they see interiors as the defining experience, not a secondary layer. That's exactly the kind of client who will appreciate depth of thinking over surface-level beauty.

For the structure, here's a framework:

**1. Opening (2-3 minutes)** — Don't start with your firm's history. They already know who you are. Start with a design philosophy statement that frames how you think about residential work.

**2. Portfolio (15-20 minutes)** — Three projects maximum, shown in depth. For each: the brief, your interpretation, the key design decisions, and the result. Show your *thinking*, not just your taste.

**3. Material Moment (5-10 minutes)** — A distinct phase where the room shifts from watching to touching. Connect each physical sample to the stories you've just told.

**4. Close** — No hard sell. End with a question that invites them to share their vision.

How does this align with your instincts?

The structure mirrors my instinct, though I would not have separated the material moment as its own phase. I tend to integrate samples throughout — pulling them forward as each project is discussed. But isolating it creates a deliberate shift in register. From looking to touching. From the visual to the tactile. That transition is powerful.

Exactly. When the family moves from sitting back and watching a screen to leaning forward and touching marble and fabric, you've changed the dynamic from audience to participant. That's where emotional connection to the work happens. The sensory shift is an event in itself, and choreographing it deliberately gives you more control of the room than letting it happen incidentally.

For the three portfolio projects — how are you selecting them? Your strongest work overall, or curated specifically for what you think the Al-Faisal family will respond to?

Curated for them, not for me. My strongest project visually is a minimalist penthouse — white plaster, concrete, restrained palette. I am proud of that work. But the Al-Faisal aesthetic, based on what I know of their properties and their social presence, leans toward a richer vocabulary. Not maximalist, but layered. Warm metals, deep stone, textiles with weight. So I am leading with a villa we completed last year — oud-toned woods, hammered brass, Calacatta marble. It speaks their language without imitating it.

That editorial decision is exactly right. You're not proving you're a good designer — they already believe that or you wouldn't have the meeting. You're proving you understand how they want to live. Showing your most personally satisfying project is tempting, but showing work that resonates with *their* sensibility is what wins the commission.

"Oud-toned woods, hammered brass, Calacatta marble" — that material palette signals cultural fluency, not just aesthetic competence. It tells them immediately that you can work at their register.

For the narrative around that villa: what's the story you'll tell? Is there a design decision in the project where you went beyond what the client asked for — something they didn't know they wanted?

There is. The family room opens onto a courtyard, and I specified a bronze mesh screen for the threshold. The obvious justification would have been privacy, but that was not my reason. I designed it so the mesh would filter the afternoon light into a geometric pattern that echoes the motifs we used in the flooring. When the sun reaches the right angle at four o'clock, the floor and the light are in conversation. The family never asked for it. They did not know they wanted it. And it is the detail they mention every single time I visit.

That is the emotional anchor of your entire presentation. The detail the client never requested but now can't stop talking about is the most powerful proof of what you offer. It demonstrates anticipation, not just execution.

In the presentation, frame it exactly as you just told it: the practical solution would have been a privacy screen. But you saw an opportunity for a dialogue between light and geometry. Show the photograph of the light hitting the floor at four o'clock. Let the image land. Then tell them: they did not ask for this, they did not know they wanted it, and now it is the thing they love most.

That single story will do more work than twenty slides of beautiful rooms. It tells the Al-Faisal family: if you hire me, I will give you things you did not know you needed. Do you have photography of that light effect?

I do. I insisted the photographer return at four o'clock specifically for that image. He thought I was being excessive. The photograph is extraordinary — the bronze light, the geometry on the stone, the warmth. It is the finest image in my portfolio.

Then that photograph anchors the first project. Place it as the final image — the reveal. Everything before it builds context: the brief, the material palette, the spatial decisions. Then this image lands as the culmination.

For the material samples on the table, make sure the bronze mesh — or a sample of it — is present alongside the marble and the wood. When you reach that moment, the family can touch the mesh while looking at the photograph. They'll feel the weight of the material and see its effect simultaneously. That cross-sensory link is almost impossible to achieve in a flat portfolio.

What about the other two projects you'll show? What role do they play in the narrative arc?

The second project is a penthouse in Jeddah with a more contemporary palette — lighter, cooler, with fluted stone and blackened steel. It demonstrates range. The Al-Faisals should know I am not limited to one register. The third is a smaller project — a private library and majlis — chosen because the scale is intimate and the craft detailing is the finest work we have done. Hand-carved plaster screens, custom joinery, calligraphic inlays. It shows depth at close range.

That's a beautifully sequenced trilogy: warmth and emotional resonance (the villa), range and modernity (the Jeddah penthouse), and then craft and intimacy (the library). Each project does different work:

- The villa says "I understand your world"
- The penthouse says "I am not bound by a single aesthetic"
- The library says "look at how closely I attend to detail"

The progression from large to intimate is also smart — it moves from the impressive to the personal, which mirrors the arc of a design relationship. You're ending on the quiet, careful work, which signals that your attention doesn't diminish at smaller scales.

For the library project especially — if you have detail photographs of the calligraphic inlays and plaster screens, hold those for a moment of slow attention. Let the room settle. The craftsmanship will do the talking.

Yes. I plan to slow the pace deliberately during the library section. Let the images breathe. No narration over every photograph — just the work, and then a sentence or two about the process. Silence is part of the composition.

Using silence as a design element in the presentation itself is a sophisticated move. It mirrors the restraint in the work and it gives the family space to have their own response rather than being narrated at. Silence signals that you trust the work to speak — which is its own kind of confidence.

What about the leave-behind books? Are those finalized?

They are at the printer. Bound in dark graphite linen with blind-embossed lettering — no foil, no gloss. The paper is uncoated cotton stock. I want the book to feel like an object of quality before they even open it. The weight in the hand should communicate something before a single page is turned.

Graphite linen, blind embossing, uncoated cotton stock — every one of those choices says something. Blind embossing over foil is a confident decision: foil announces itself, blind embossing asks you to look closer. The restraint is the message.

Clients at this level handle luxury objects constantly. They know the difference between something considered and something produced. Your book will feel considered.

One detail if it's not too late: a personalized element in each book. A handwritten note on the inside cover, or their name set into the title page. It signals this was prepared for them specifically.

Each book will have a handwritten note from me on the title page. I write with a Montblanc on heavy stock — the ink sits beautifully on cotton paper. The note will be brief. Something to the effect of how it would be an honour to shape the spaces where their family gathers. Personal without being effusive. I do not grovel.

"Personal without being effusive" — that's exactly the right register. A single handwritten sentence from a Montblanc on cotton stock carries more weight than a paragraph of flattery. It says you value their time the same way you value a clean composition. And the tactile quality of pen on that paper will register immediately.

You have every element in place: the portfolio is curated for their aesthetic, the material samples create a tactile bridge, the bronze mesh photograph is your emotional anchor, the leave-behinds are objects of quality, and the structure gives you control of the room without making it feel scripted. March 5th. You are prepared.

Inshallah. I have done everything within my control. The rest is not mine to decide. But I will walk into that room knowing that every detail has been considered, every material chosen with intention, and every word weighed. That is all I can offer — and it is enough.

It is more than enough. The Al-Faisal family will experience a presentation that was designed, not assembled — and that coherence between what you say, what you show, and what they hold in their hands is rare. It reflects the same sensibility you bring to the spaces themselves. Best of luck on March 5th.
